elements.sent-2411-001: Recent observations by pulsar timing arrays (PTAs) indicate a potential detection of a stochastic gravitational wave (GW) background.
elements.sent-2411-002: Metastable cosmic strings have been recognized as a possible source of the observed signals.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we propose an $R$-invariant supersymmetric new inflation model.
elements.sent-2411-004: It is characterized by a two-step symmetry breaking $\mathrm{SU (2) \to \mathrm{U (1)_G \to \mathrm{nothing}$, incorporating metastable cosmic strings.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: The field responsible for the initial symmetry breaking acts as the inflaton, while the second symmetry breaking occurs post-inflation, ensuring the formation of the cosmic string network without monopole production.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our model predicts symmetry breaking scales consistent with the string tensions favored by PTA data, $G_\mathrm{N \mu_\mathrm{str \sim 10^{-5}$, where $G_\mathrm{N}$ is the Newton constant.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Notably, a low reheating temperature is required to suppress non-thermal gravitino production from the decay of inflaton sector fields.
elements.sent-2411-008: This also helps evading LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA constraints, while yielding a distinctive GW signature that future PTA and interferometer experiments can detect.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, we examine the consistency of this scenario with non-thermal leptogenesis and supersymmetric dark matter.
elements.sent-2411-010: Recently, pulsar timing array (PTA) collaborations reported an observation of the Hellings-Downs angular correlation in the nanohertz frequency range, suggesting evidence for a stochastic gravitational wave (GW) signal~\cite{NANOGrav:2023gor,EPTA:2023fyk,Reardon:2023gzh,Xu:2023wog .}
elements.sent-2411-011: From the perspective of new physics searches, stochastic GWs have served as a vital observational channel for investigating cosmic strings (for reviews, see, e.g., Refs.\,\cite{Vilenkin:1982hm,Hindmarsh:2011qj,Auclair:2019wcv,Gouttenoire:2019kij ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Cosmic strings are topological defects that emerge from a $\uone$ symmetry breaking, with their stability ensured by the non-trivial topological charge $\pi_1\qty[\mathrm{U (1)] = \mathbb{Z}$.}
elements.sent-2411-013: These strings form a network that exhibits scaling behavior, maintaining a correlation length on the order of the Hubble length at all times while generating loops through reconnection.
elements.sent-2411-014: The resulting cosmic string loops gradually contract by emitting GWs until they eventually vanish, thereby contributing to the stochastic GW background.
elements.sent-2411-015: Interestingly, the observed GW spectrum appears to favor a metastable cosmic string network rather than a stable one~\cite{NANOGrav:2023hvm .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In the case of metastable cosmic strings, they decay in the later Universe, causing a suppression in the low-frequency GW spectrum (see Refs.~\cite{Leblond:2009fq,Buchmuller:2019gfy,Buchmuller:2020lbh,Buchmuller:2021mbb,Buchmuller:2023aus for further details).}
elements.sent-2411-017: This suppression in the low-frequency region provides a better fit to the PTA signal than a stable string network.
elements.sent-2411-018: These recent PTA observations have thus sparked considerable interest in metastable cosmic strings.
elements.sent-2411-019: Metastable strings are predicted in models that undergo a two-step symmetry breaking, such as: \begin{equation}G \rightarrow U(1) \rightarrow nothing \ ,\end{equation} where the vacuum expectation values (VEVs) are hierarchical.
elements.sent-2411-020: In such models, $\pi_1\qty[G]$ is trivial while $\pi_2\qty[G/\mathrm{U (1)] = \pi_1[\mathrm{U (1)] = \mathbb{Z}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this setup, cosmic strings emerge as classically stable configurations due to the non-trivial topological charge of $\pi_1[\mathrm{U (1)]$ in the low-energy effective theory below the $G \to \mathrm{U (1)$ symmetry-breaking scale.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, in the full theory, cosmic strings are ultimately metastable since $\pi_1[G] = 0$, meaning no topological charge protects them.
elements.sent-2411-023: More closely, these models feature monopole configurations associated with the topological charge $\pi_2[G/\mathrm{U (1)]$ at the first symmetry breaking.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The breaking of the strings can be understood as the Schwinger effect~\cite{Preskill:1992ck , which generates monopole-antimonopole pairs within the string, leading to the fragmentation of long strings into smaller pieces.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Notably, the string breaking rate inferred from the PTA signal suggests that the mass of the magnetic monopole $m_M$ (of order of the first symmetry breaking scale) and the cosmic string tension $\mu_\mathrm{str}$ (of order of the second symmetry breaking scale) are not hierarchically separated, i.e., $m_M \sim \sqrt{\mu_\mathrm{str }$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This raises questions about whether the symmetry breaking occurs in a sequential manner that allows for the formation of a long cosmic string network as anticipated above.
elements.sent-2411-027: It is also unclear whether the monopoles generated at the first symmetry-breaking stage are sufficiently diluted to avoid the monopole problem.
elements.sent-2411-028: In light of the PTA observations, there has been significant discussion focusing on realistic models and cosmological scenarios that lead to the formation of metastable strings, particularly within the framework of supersymmetric Grand Unified Theories (GUTs)~\cite{Buchmuller:2021dtt,Ahmed:2023pjl,Afzal:2023cyp,Lazarides:2023rqf,Buchmuller:2024zzk,Maji:2024cwv,Ahmed:2024iyd,Pallis:2024mip .}
elements.sent-2411-029: There are clear reasons for this interest.
elements.sent-2411-030: First, the PTA signal not only suggests that the scales of the two-step symmetry breaking are close to each other but also favors scales around the GUT scale, i.e., $10^{\numrange{15 {16 }$ GeV.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, the mechanism of GUT symmetry breaking is highly compatible with hybrid inflation, leading to the proposal of various models incorporating this framework.
elements.sent-2411-032: In such scenarios, however, both the monopole-generating symmetry breaking and the U(1) symmetry breaking occur after inflation, necessitating further investigation of the monopoles and the cosmic string network formation processes.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this paper, we propose an alternative inflationary scenario that accommodates a successful picture of metastable cosmic strings, where the first symmetry-breaking field (i.e., the $G$-breaking field) serves as the inflaton (see Refs.\,\cite{Pallis:2024joc,Antusch:2024qpb ).}
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, we focus on an $R$-symmetric supersymmetric new inflation model~\cite{Izawa:1996dv,Izawa:1997df .}
elements.sent-2411-035: An advantage of this type of model is that the symmetry enhancement point is naturally selected as the initial condition through pre-inflationary dynamics~\cite{Izawa:1997df .}
elements.sent-2411-036: We consider the two-step symmetry breaking of $G=\SU(2)$ gauge symmetry to $\uone$ by an $\SU(2)$ adjoint field, which plays the role of the inflaton in new inflation model.
elements.sent-2411-037: The remaining $\uone$ symmetry is then broken by $\SU(2)$ doublet fields~\cite{Kephart:1995cg .}
elements.sent-2411-038: In this setup, $\SU(2)$ symmetry is broken at the onset of inflation, whereas $\uone$ symmetry breaking occurs post-inflation.
elements.sent-2411-039: As a result, the cosmic string network is formed after inflation while monopoles are diluted during inflation, ensuring they do not interfere with the formation of the cosmic string network.
elements.sent-2411-040: As a characteristic feature, the model predicts a string tension of order of the GUT scale.
elements.sent-2411-041: Besides, it turns out that the model requires a low reheating temperature after inflation to avoid excessive gravitino production from the decay of inflaton sector fields.
elements.sent-2411-042: Interestingly, this low reheating temperature enables the GW spectrum to be consistent with the constraints set by the LIGO–Virgo–KAGRA (LVK) collaboration~\cite{KAGRA:2021kbb , even for a relatively high string tension at the GUT scale, i.e., $G_{\mathrm{N \mu_\mathrm{str \sim 10^{-5}$, where $G_{\mathrm{N }$ denotes the Newton constant.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: We also provide a working example for a successful cosmology, where the baryon asymmetry of the Universe is provided by the non-thermal leptogenesis and the dark matter is provided by the lightest supersymmetric particle (LSP).
elements.sent-2411-044: We demonstrate that these scenarios, along with the present inflation model, are all consistent within the framework of a high-scale supersymmetry model featuring PeV-scale gravitino/sfermion masses and TeV-scale anomaly-mediated gaugino masses.
elements.sent-2411-045: The organization of this paper is as follows: In \cref{sec:Model , we summarize the model of supersymmetric $R$-invariant new inflation, where the symmetry-breaking field acts as the inflaton.}
elements.sent-2411-046: In \cref{sec:inflation , we analyze the model, determining the model parameters that reproduce the observed curvature perturbations and spectral index of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) anisotropies.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In \cref{sec:reheating , we discuss the post inflation dynamics and reheating process addressing the gravitino problem, dark matter, and non-thermal leptogenesis.}
elements.sent-2411-048: In \cref{sec:CSGWS , we explore the properties of cosmic strings and present the predicted GW spectrum.}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Symmetry Breaking Pattern and Summary of Model
elements.sent-2411-050: Before discussing the details, let us summarize features of the model.
elements.sent-2411-051: We consider a supersymmetric $\SU(2)$ gauge theory where $\SU(2)$ is broken down to a $\uone$ gauge symmetry by an $\SU(2)$ adjoint chiral superfield $\phi^a$ $(a=1,2,3)$.
elements.sent-2411-052: We refer to this residual $\uone$ gauge symmetry as the $\uoneG$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-053: The residual $\uoneG$ gauge symmetry is broken by a pair of doublet chiral superfields $H=(H_1,H_2)$ and $\bar{H =(\bar{H _1,\bar{H _2)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Hereafter, we assume the VEVs of these fields are given by \begin{equation}\expval{\phi_3}=v_{\SU(2)} \ , \quad \expval{H_1} = \expval{\bar{H}_1} = v_{U(1)}\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: In the following, we construct a model of new inflation where $\phi_3$ plays the role of the inflaton.
elements.sent-2411-056: We assume a gravitino mass in the PeV range, where supersymmetry breaking occurs in a dynamical sector separated from the inflaton sector.
elements.sent-2411-057: As we will see, the Hubble parameter during inflation is $H_\mathrm{inf =\order{10^{10 }$\,GeV.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The dynamical scale of the supersymmetry breaking is set to $\order{10^{\numrange{12 {13 }$\,GeV for PeV-scale gravitino mass, and thus, we assume it has occurred before the onset of the inflation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: We will also see that the soft supersymmetry-breaking masses are smaller than the supersymmetric masses in the inflaton sector, and hence, the soft masses do not affect the inflation dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-060: Note also that the energy density of the supersymmetry-breaking sector to the inflation is subdominant.
elements.sent-2411-061: As we mentioned above, the supersymmetry breaking has taken place before inflation.
elements.sent-2411-062: At the end of inflation the adjoint field obtains the VEV, $v_{\mathrm{SU (2)}$, and the doublets obtain the VEV, $v_{\mathrm{U (1)}$, which are both of $\order{10^{16 }$\,GeV.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The masses of the inflaton sector fields are of $\order{10^{\numrange{10 {12 }$\,GeV (see \cref{sec:reference ).}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-005: Remaining U(1) Symmetry Breaking after Inflation
elements.sent-2411-132: During inflation, $H$ and $\bar{H}$ stay at the origin by assuming the positive Hubble mass terms, as we discuss later (see \cref{eq:mHeff ).}

elements.sent-2411-134: Notice that the superpotential term proportional to $\zeta$ in \cref{eq:Wpot leads to a scalar potential of $H=\bar{H}$, \begin{equation}V_{\zeta} \sim \frac{ \zeta^2 \expval{\phi_3}^2} {M_Pl^{4n-8}} \qty| H^{4n-6}|\ .\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-136: To avoid this, let us compare $V_\zeta$ with the destabilization terms of $H$ and $\bar{H}$ in the scalar potential \begin{equation}V_{destabilize} = -\lambda_Y \frac{v_Y^2(\bar{H}H)^{n}}{M_{Pl}^{2n-2}} + \hc \ ,\end{equation} which drives $H$ and $\bar{H}$ to the supersymmetric vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-137: For $n>3$, $V_\zeta$ is more suppressed than $V_\mathrm{destabilize}$, and hence, $V_\zeta$ does not lead to a local minimum.

elements.sent-2411-140: As a result, we find that $H$ and $\bar{H}$ smoothly roll down to the supersymmetric vacuum after inflation for $n\ge 3$.

elements.subsection-2411-008: Stabilization of Fields Other than Inflaton
elements.sent-2411-182: In the above discussion, we have assumed that only the inflaton has a non-vanishing field value during inflation.
elements.sent-2411-183: Now, let us discuss other fields are stabilized at around $X=Y=H=\bar{H =0$.}
elements.sent-2411-184: The potential of $H$ and $\bar{H}$ is given by \begin{equation}V_{H,\bar{H}}= \qty| v_X^2-\lambda_X \frac{(\phi\cdot\phi)^n}{M_{Pl}^{2n-2}}|^2 \times \qty((1-k_H)\frac{H^\dagger H}{M_Pl^2} + (1-k_{\bar{H}})\frac{\bar{H}^\dagger \bar{H}}{M_Pl^2}) \cr +\qty| v_Y^2-\lambda_Y\frac{(\bar{H}H)^n}{M_Pl^{2n-2}}|^2\times \qty(1+(1-f_H)\frac{H^\dagger H}{M_Pl^2}+(1-f_{\bar{H}})\frac{\bar{H}^\dagger \bar{H}}{M_Pl^2}) -v_Y^4 \cr +\qty(\qty| v_Y^2-\lambda_Y\frac{(\bar{H}H)^n}{M_Pl^{2n-2}}|^2-v_Y^4)\times (1-f_\phi)\frac{\phi^\dagger \cdot \phi}{M_Pl^2}\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-185: Here, we have neglected the contribution from the superpotential term proportional to $\zeta$ since they are suppressed.
elements.sent-2411-186: We have also omitted the soft supersymmetry breaking masses of $H$ and $\bar{H}$, since the Hubble rate $H_\mathrm{inf}$ is much larger.
elements.sent-2411-187: The above potential leads to the effective mass terms around $H=\bar{H =0$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:mHeff} m_{H,\bar{H}}^2 =\frac{1}{M_Pl^2} \qty| v_X^2-\lambda_X \frac{(\phi\cdot\phi)^n}{M_{Pl}^{2n-2}}|^2 (1-k_{H,\bar{H}}) + \frac{1}{M_Pl^2}v_Y^4(1-f_{H,H})\ .\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-253: In this section, we discuss the reheating process of the inflaton sector.
elements.sent-2411-254: The reheating process in the present model is somewhat complex, as seen below.
elements.sent-2411-255: For illustrative purposes, we show a schematic time evolution of the energy densities in \cref{fig:rhohistory .}
elements.sent-2411-256: The decay rates of the inflaton sector fields will be explained in \cref{sec:decay .}
elements.sent-2411-257: The typical time evolution is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-258: After inflation, the inflaton $\phi_3$ begins oscillating while other fields are stabilized around their origins.
elements.sent-2411-259: When the inflaton oscillation energy density decreases down to $v_Y^4$, other fields start to oscillate.
elements.sent-2411-260: The reheating process takes place at much lower energy density determined by the decay rate of the lightest inflaton sector fields, $\chi_{1,2}$.
elements.sent-2411-261: The other inflaton sector fields $\phi_3$, $X$, $\chi_3$ and $Y$ have decayed into a pair of $\chi_{1,2}$'s well before the completion of the reheating.
elements.sent-2411-262: As an important note, the inflaton sector fields obtain large VEVs, and hence, the inflaton sector fields other than $\chi_{1,2}$ have sizable branching fraction into a pair of the gravitinos~\cite{Endo:2006zj,Nakamura:2006uc,Endo:2006tf,Kawasaki:2006gs,Dine:2006ii,Asaka:2006bv,Kawasaki:2006hm,Endo:2006qk,Nakayama:2012hy .}
elements.sent-2411-263: As we will see in Sec\,\ref{sec:gravitino , the abundance of non-thermally produced gravitinos puts a severe upper limit on the reheating temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-264: Before discussing the thermal history in detail, we first review the setup of the supersymmetric standard model (SSM).
elements.sent-2411-265: Here, we assume the gravitino mass is of order PeV.
elements.sent-2411-266: Under this assumption, we outline the SSM within the high-scale supersymmetry framework~\cite{Hall:2011jd, Hall:2012zp, Nomura:2014asa, Ibe:2011aa, Ibe:2012hu, Arvanitaki:2012ps, ArkaniHamed:2012gw characterized by a PeV-scale gravitino mass.}
elements.sent-2411-267: A primary advantage of a PeV-scale gravitino scenario is that it generates TeV-scale gaugino masses via anomaly-mediated supersymmetry breaking (AMSB), independent of specific details of dynamical supersymmetry-breaking sector~\cite{Randall:1998uk, Giudice:1998xp .}
elements.sent-2411-268: This feature, which eliminates the need for a singlet supersymmetry-breaking field, is crucial in avoiding the cosmological Polonyi problem~\cite{Coughlan:1983ci,Goncharov:1984qm,Ellis:1986zt .}
elements.sent-2411-269: This should be contrasted with the gravity mediation models, where the Polonyi problem persists even if supersymmetry breaking is dynamical and occurs through strong coupling~\cite{Ibe:2006am .}
elements.sent-2411-270: In addition, it is also advantageous that the PeV-scale gravitino decays well before the Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN), preserving successful BBN predictions.
elements.sent-2411-271: Typical sfermion masses for a PeV-scale gravitino are of the same order as the gravitino mass.
elements.sent-2411-272: These heavy sfermions can easily explain the observed Higgs boson mass of $125$\,GeV~\cite{Okada:1990vk,Okada:1991jc,Ellis:1990nz,Haber:1990aw,Ellis:1991zd .}
elements.sent-2411-273: With these considerations, we adopt the following high-scale supersymmetry spectrum: \begin{equation}\label{eq:PeVSUSY} m_gaugino = \order{1}\,TeV, \quad m_sfermion \simeq m_{3/2} = \order{100}\,TeV - \order{1}\,PeV\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-274: A notable feature of the AMSB model is that the Wino, the SU(2)$_L$ gaugino in the SSM, typically becomes the lightest supersymmetric particle (LSP).
elements.sent-2411-275: In what follows, we consider Wino LSP as an example; however, the current inflation model is also compatible with a Higgsino LSP or a more general neutralino LSP with mixed Higgsino/Bino/Wino content.
elements.subsection-2411-011: Decay of Inflaton Sector Fields and Reheating Process

elements.section-2411-005: Cosmic String and Gravitational Wave Signal
elements.sent-2411-375: As discussed in the previous section, the $\SU(2)$ symmetry is broken during inflation, while the $\uone_G$ symmetry breaking occurs after inflation.
elements.sent-2411-376: Consequently, the string network associated with the breaking of the $\uone_G$ symmetry is formed.
elements.sent-2411-377: In this section, we explore the characteristics of the cosmic strings in this model and the GW signals produced by this string network.
elements.sent-2411-378: Before proceeding further, it is important to note theoretical uncertainties in the GW spectrum generated by the metastable string.
elements.sent-2411-379: In models where the monopole at the endpoint of the metastable string has unconfined magnetic flux associated with an unbroken $\uone$ gauge symmetry, the string segments dissipate most of their energy by emitting massless gauge bosons shortly after their formation~\cite{Berezinsky:1997kd .}

elements.sent-2411-455: In this paper, we discussed a supersymmetric new inflation model that can generate a metastable string network through two-step symmetry breaking, $\SU(2)\to \uone_G \to nothing$.
elements.sent-2411-456: In this model, the inflaton corresponds to the field responsible for the first step of two-step symmetry breaking.
elements.sent-2411-457: Accordingly, the first symmetry breaking occurs at the onset of inflation, while the second breaking takes place after inflation.
elements.sent-2411-458: This set up allows the formation of the string network without monopole production.
elements.sent-2411-459: We also find that the model can successfully provide the GUT scale breaking scales favored by the metastable string interpretation of the PTA GW signal.
elements.sent-2411-460: We have also discussed the decay processes of the inflaton sector fields in detail.
elements.sent-2411-461: Then, we found that the model requires rather low reheating temperature to avoid the gravitino overproduction from the decays of the inflaton sector fields.
elements.sent-2411-462: Interestingly, this low reheating temperature enables the GW spectrum to be consistent with LVK constraints for a relatively high string tension $G_{\mathrm{N \mu_\mathrm{str \sim 10^{-5}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-463: The characteristic stochastic GW spectrum predicted by this model presented in \cref{fig:spectrum can be tested by future PTA experiments \cite{Carilli:2004nx, Janssen:2014dka, Weltman:2018zrl and interferometer experiments \cite{Seto:2001qf, Kawamura:2006up, Yagi:2011wg, Isoyama:2018rjb, Crowder:2005nr, Corbin:2005ny, Harry:2006fi, Somiya:2011np, Aso:2013eba, KAGRA:2018plz, KAGRA:2019htd, Michimura:2019cvl, LIGOScientific:2016wof, Reitze:2019iox, Punturo:2010zz, Hild:2010id, Sathyaprakash:2012jk, ET:2019dnz (see also Ref.\,\cite{Schmitz:2020syl for theoretical study of the future sensitivities).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-464: To provide a working example for the successful cosmology, we also discussed the non-thermal leptogenesis as well as the LSP dark matter scenario.
elements.sent-2411-465: Our model proved consistent with these scenarios with PeV-scale gravitino/sfermion masses and the anomaly-mediated gaugino masses in the TeV range.
elements.sent-2411-466: Notice that several challenges remain unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-467: Firstly, in this work, we assumed that the cosmic strings generated by the $\uone_G$ symmetry breaking are classically stable.
elements.sent-2411-468: However, since the $\uone_G$-breaking VEV is close to the $\SU(2)$-breaking VEV, it is non-trivial whether the static string configuration has no instabilities such as $W$-condensation~\cite{Ambjorn:1989sz .}
elements.sent-2411-469: It is also important to explore the possibility of monopole-anitmonopole pair creation during string reconnection.
elements.sent-2411-470: Besides, the cosmic strings considered here are much thicker than the inverse of the mass of the $\uone_G$ gauge boson (see \cref{fig:string_configuration ), requiring a more precise analysis of the string-breaking rate via monopole pair production.}
elements.sent-2411-471: This would refine the approximation used in Ref.\,\cite{Preskill:1992ck , which neglected the string thickness and monopole size (see, e.g., Refs.\,\cite{Shifman:2002yi,Chitose:2023dam ).}}
elements.sent-2411-472: Furthermore, although we utilized the VOS model calibrated for Nambu-Goto strings, the string thickness may also influence the evolution of the cosmic string network.
elements.sent-2411-473: These are left for future investigation.
elements.sent-2411-474: The evolution of the string segments in the present model is also an open question, since the metastable string segments are associated with the $\uone_{B-L}$ magnetic field which is confined along the segments.
elements.sent-2411-475: In particular, computation of the GW spectrum requires understanding of frictions from SM plasma through which monopoles lose energy.
elements.sent-2411-476: Besides, such energy release through the confined $\uone_{B-L}$ magnetic flux at the cosmic temperature below $1$\,MeV could have detectable effects on the BBN and CMB.
elements.sent-2411-477: We will study those effects of the $\uone_{B-L}$ magnetic field in future work.
elements.sent-2411-478: Also, the post-inflation dynamics must be investigated more in detail.
elements.sent-2411-479: As we discussed in \cref{sec:reheating , the energy fractions of the inflaton sector fields in the early matter domination are given by the ensemble average over many Hubble patches since they are influenced by the Hubble fluctuations.}
elements.sent-2411-480: Since the gravitino abundance depends on them, statistical analysis is required to narrow down the viable parameter space of this model.

elements.section-2411-008: Asymptotic Behavior of Gravitational Wave Spectrum
elements.sent-2411-494: In this appendix, we discuss the asymptotic behavior of the GW energy density spectrum for a large frequency $f$.
elements.sent-2411-495: The GW energy density spectrum is given by, \begin{equation}\label{eq:OmegaGW} \Omega_GW(f) = \frac{f}{\rho_c} \dv{\rho_GW(f,t_0)}{f}\ , \cr = \sum_{k\ge 1}\frac{1}{\rho_c} \frac{2k}{f} \frac{\sqrt{2}F_\alpha P_k}{\gamma_\alpha\alpha(\alpha + \Gamma G_N \mu_str )} \int_{t_form}^{t_0} dt \qty(\frac{a(t)}{a(t_0)})^{5}\qty(\frac{a(t_i)}{a(t)})^3 \frac{C_eff(t_i)}{t_i^4} \cr \phantom{XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX}\times \Theta(t-t_i)\Theta(t_i - t_osc) \Theta(t_i - \ell_*/\alpha) \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-496: The derivation of this expression follows Ref.\,\cite{Gouttenoire:2019kij , and the notation for parameters is also generally consistent with the reference.}
elements.sent-2411-497: The GW spectrum is obtained by integrating over the emission time $t$ from the formation time of the string network $t_\mathrm{form}$ to the present Universe $t_0$.
elements.sent-2411-498: The third step function ensures that the loop length is sufficiently large so that particle production from cusps to become negligible~\cite{Gouttenoire:2019kij .}
elements.sent-2411-499: However, these step functions are not relevant for discussing the asymptotic behavior addressed in this appendix.
elements.sent-2411-500: The scale factor is represented by $a(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-501: The parameter $\alpha$ represents the string loop length normalized by the cosmic time at the production $t_i$, such that $\ell = \alpha t_i$.
elements.sent-2411-502: The quantities $\mathcal{F _\alpha$ and $\gamma_\alpha$ denote the fraction of loops and their corresponding boost factor, respectively, for loops formed with the length $\ell = \alpha t_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-503: The loop formation efficiency, $C_\mathrm{eff (t)$, is determined by solving the VOS equations for a given loop-production coefficient $\tilde{c}$, a parameter in the VOS model.}
elements.sent-2411-504: In the scaling regime of the string network, $C_\mathrm{eff (t)$ becomes a constant in time, \begin{equation}\label{eq:Ceff} C_eff|_{\tilde{c}=0.23} \simeq  5.4 p=4\,(RD) 0.39 p=3\,(MD) \ , \qquad C_eff|_{\tilde{c}=0.57} \simeq  1.3 p=4\,(RD) 0.23 p=3\,(MD) \ ,\end{equation} for the RD and MD eras, respectively.}
elements.formula-2411-126: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Ceff} C_eff|_{\tilde{c}=0.23} \simeq  5.4 p=4\,(RD) 0.39 p=3\,(MD) \ , \qquad C_eff|_{\tilde{c}=0.57} \simeq  1.3 p=4\,(RD) 0.23 p=3\,(MD) \ ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-505: Here, the case $\tilde{c}\simeq 0.23$ corresponds to the VOS model calibrated to match Nambu-Goto simulations~\cite{Martins:2000cs while $\tilde{c}\simeq 0.57$ to the VOS model calibrated to Abelian-Higgs simulations~\cite{Martins:2000cs .}}
elements.sent-2411-506: For a given GW frequency $f$ and harmonic mode $k \in \mathbb{N _+$, the loop production time is given by, \begin{equation}\label{eq:tprod} t_i(f,t) = \frac{1}{\alpha + \Gamma G_N \mu_str }\qty[\frac{2k}{f}\qty(\frac{a(\temit)}{a(t_0)}) + \Gamma G_N \mu_str\times \temit]\ .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-507: The frequency dependence in \cref{eq:OmegaGW appears only through the combination $k/f$.}
elements.sent-2411-508: The harmonic mode dependence, on the other hand, also appears through the GW power spectrum $P_k$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:Pk} P_k = \frac{\Gamma G_N\mu_str^2} {\zeta_R(4/3)}k^{-4/3}\ , \quad \zeta_R(s)= \sum_{k\ge 1} k^{-s}\ ,\end{equation} where we assume that the GW's from the string network are dominated by those from the cusps on the string loops~\,\cite{Blanco-Pillado:2017oxo .}
elements.sent-2411-509: Now, let us consider the asymptotic behavior of the integrand of \cref{eq:OmegaGW .}
elements.sent-2411-510: In the RD era, it asymptotes to \begin{equation}\lim_{t\to \infty} \qty(\frac{a(t)}{a(t_0)})^{5}\qty(\frac{a(t_i)}{a(t)})^3 \frac{1}{t_i^4}\bigg|_RD \propto t^{-3/2}\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-511: Besides, the $\temit$ integration is dominated by the contribution from the region satisfying \begin{equation}\label{eq:temitRD} \frac{2k}{f}\qty(\frac{t_emit}{t_0})^{1/2} \sim \Gamma G_N \mu_str\times t_emit\ ,\end{equation} and hence $t_\mathrm{emit \propto (f/k)^{-2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-512: Accordingly, in the RD era, the frequency dependence of the GW spectrum for a given harmonic mode $\Omega_{\mathrm{GW ^{(k)}$ asymptotes to \begin{equation}\label{eq:OmegaKRD} \Omega^{(k)}_{GW}|_RD \propto \frac{k}{f} \times t_emit^{-1/2}\times P_k \propto \qty(\frac{k}{f})^0 \times P_k\ .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-513: This explains why the GW spectrum forms a plateau in the high-frequency region as in \cref{eq:plateau .}
elements.sent-2411-514: In the MD era, on the other hand, the integrand of \cref{eq:OmegaGW asymptotes to \begin{equation}\lim_{t\to \infty} \qty(\frac{a(t)}{a(t_0)})^{5}\qty(\frac{a(t_i)}{a(t)})^3 \frac{1}{t_i^4}\bigg|_MD \propto t^{-2/3}\ .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-515: Thus, the $\temit$ integration is dominated by the region where \begin{equation}\frac{2k}{f}\qty(\frac{t_emit}{t_0})^{1/2} \ll \Gamma G_N \mu_str\times t_emit \ ,\end{equation} and hence, the integrated value in \cref{eq:OmegaGW becomes independent of $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-516: Thus, in the MD era, the frequency dependence of $\Omega_\mathrm{GW ^{(k)}$ asymptotes to \begin{equation}\label{eq:OmegaKMD} \Omega^{(k)}_{GW}|_MD \propto \frac{k}{f} \times P_k \ .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-517: Finally, let us discuss the summation over the harmonic mode.
elements.sent-2411-518: Consequently, the total differential GWs emitted in the early MD era is proportional to \begin{equation}\label{eq:OmegaGWkmax} \Omega_GW|_MD \propto \frac{1}{f} \times \sum_{k=1}^{k_max} k^{-1/3} \sim \frac{1}{f} \times k_max^{2/3} \ ,\end{equation} we have used \cref{eq:Pk .}
elements.sent-2411-519: By noting that the harmonic modes with $k$ satisfying \begin{equation}f > f^{(k)}(T_R)\ ,\end{equation} are emitted in the MD era, the $k_\mathrm{max}$ in \cref{eq:OmegaGWkmax is given by $k_*$, \begin{equation}f = f^{(k_*)}(T_R)\ .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-520: Thus, since $k_*\propto f$, we find that the total GW spectrum is suppressed by \begin{equation}\label{eq:OmegaGWMD} \Omega_GW|_MD \propto f^{-1/3} \ ,\end{equation} for $f \gg f^{(1) (T_R)$.}
elements.sent-2411-521: Here, supergravity effects and non-minimal couplings in the Kähler potential are ignored.
elements.sent-2411-523: Also, we employ the unit system with $M_{\mathrm{Pl =1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-524: Plugging the ansatzes \eqref{eq:string ansatz1 , \eqref{eq:string ansatzX , \eqref{eq:string ansatzY and \eqref{eq:string ansatz2 and $X\equiv Y\equiv 1$ into the action, we find the tension to be \begin{equation}\mu_\text{str}=2\pi \int_0^\infty d\rho\,\rho \Big[ \frac{2}{g^2\rho^2}(\partial_\rho a_s)^2 +2\vone^2\pqty{(\partial_\rho h_s)^2+\frac{1}{\rho^2}a_s^2h_s^2} +\lambda_Y^2 \vone^{12}(1-h_s^6)^2 \Big]\ .\label{eq:simpletension}\end{equation} The equations of motion are \begin{equation}\partial_\rho^2h_s+\frac{1}{\rho}\partial_\rho h_s-\frac{1}{\rho^2}a_s^2h_s+3M^2h_s^5(1-h_s^6)=0\ , \label{eq:heom} \partial_\rho^2 a_s-\frac{1}{\rho}\partial_\rho a_s -g^2\vone^2 a_sh_s^2=0\ ,\label{eq:aeom}\end{equation} where $M:=\lambda_Y\vone^5$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-525: Since the potential is suppressed by $M^2\propto M_{\mathrm{Pl ^{-10}$, the situation is close to the type-I Abrikosov-Nielsen-Olesen string~\cite{Abrikosov:1956sx,Nielsen:1973cs .}}}
elements.sent-2411-526: Thus, we may proceed by mimicking the argument by Yung~\cite{Yung:1999du .}
elements.sent-2411-527: We suppose the gauge field is confined in a small radius $\rho_g$, whereas $h_s$ start to grow when $\rho>\rho_g$.
elements.sent-2411-528: Our ansatz for $a_s$ is \begin{equation}a_s(\rho)=\begin{cases} 1-\frac{\rho^2}{\rho_g^2} (\rho\ll \rho_g) 0 (\rho\gg \rho_g) \end{cases}\end{equation} The branch for small $\rho$ satisfies \cref{eq:aeom with $h_s=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-530: We employ different approximations in two regions of $\rho$ and glue them together.
elements.sent-2411-531: For $h_s\ll 1$, the potential term can be neglected.
elements.sent-2411-532: The solution is \begin{equation}h_s(\rho)=C\ln\frac{\rho}{\rho_g}\ ,\end{equation} where $C$ is an integral constant and we required that $h_s(\rho_g)=0$.
elements.sent-2411-533: For this approximation to be valid, \begin{equation}\partial_\rho^2 h_s\sim \frac{1}{\rho}\partial_\rho h_s\sim \frac{C}{\rho^2}\gg 3M^2h_s^5(1-h_s^6)>M^2,\end{equation} or \begin{equation}\rho^2\ll \frac{C}{M^2}\end{equation} is required.
elements.sent-2411-536: This can be solved as \begin{equation}\tilde{h}_s(\rho)=BK_0(3\sqrt{2}M\rho)\ ,\end{equation} where $B$ is another integral constant and $K_0$ is the modified Bessel function of the second kind.
elements.sent-2411-537: Note that $I_0$, the modified Bessel function of the first kind, is incompatible with the boundary condition at $\rho\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-538: The $\order{\tilde{h _s^2}$ term in \cref{eq:heom is $-45M^2\tilde{h _s^2$, and thus, the range of validity is $\tilde{h _s<\order{0.1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-539: We must join the two at some $\rho=\rho_0$, where both the approximations are applicable.
elements.sent-2411-540: We will see later that $\rho_0$ can be chosen so that $3\sqrt{2 M\rho_0 \lesssim \order{0.1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-541: Consequently, the asymptotic form for small $z$ \begin{equation}K_0(z)\simeq -\ln \frac{z}{2}-\gE\end{equation} is valid, where $\gE\approx 0.577$.
elements.sent-2411-542: Requiring $h_s(\rho_0)$ and $h_s'(\rho_0)$ to agree on the both sides, we have \begin{equation}B=C=\bqty{\ln\frac{\rho_M}{\rho_g}}^{-1}\ ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\rho_M:=\frac{\sqrt{2}e^{-\gE}}{3M}\ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-543: The contribution to $\mu_{\mathrm{str }$ from $\rho<\rho_g$ is \begin{equation}{\mu_{\mathrm{str}}}|_{\rho<\rho_g}=2\pi\int_0^{\rho_g}d\rho\,\rho \frac{2}{g^2\rho^2}(\partial_\rho a_s)^2=\frac{8\pi}{g^2\rho_g^2}\ . \label{eq:smalltension}\end{equation} The contribution from $\rho>\rho_g$ is \begin{equation}{\mu_{\mathrm{str}}}|_{\rho>\rho_g}=2\pi\int_{\rho_g}^{\rho_0}d\rho\,\rho \bqty{2\vone(\partial_\rho h_s)^2+\vone^2M^2(1-h_s^6)}\simeq 4\pi \vone^2 \pqty{\ln\frac{\rho_M}{\rho_g}}^{-1}\ , \label{eq:largetension}\end{equation} which is saturated by the kinetic term of $h_s$.}
elements.sent-2411-544: Requiring $\rho_g$ to minimize the total tension, we get \begin{equation}\frac{\rho_M}{\rho_g}\simeq \frac{g\vone\rho_M}{2}\pqty{\ln\frac{g\vone\rho_M}{2}}^{-1} \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-545: Substituting this into Eqs.~\eqref{eq:smalltension and \eqref{eq:largetension , we get the final result \begin{equation}\mu_{\mathrm{str}}\simeq 4\pi \vone^2 \bqty{\ln( \frac{g\vone\rho_M}{2}\pqty{\ln\frac{g\vone\rho_M}{2}}^{-1})}^{-1}\ . \label{eq:approxtension}\end{equation} Substituting the values of BP$_1$ ($\vone=0.03$, $\lambda_Y=0.1$ and $g=1$), we have $\mu_{\mathrm{str /(2\pi \vone^2)\simeq0.170$, in reasonable agreement with the right side of \cref{fig:string_configuration .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-546: Also, choosing $3\sqrt{2 M\rho_0=0.35$ gives $\rho_0^2/(C/M^2)=0.080$ and $h_s(\rho_0)=0.90$, satisfying all the requirements.. \bibliographystyle{apsrev4-1.}}
